meta.title: SF-SPSE-1: Identity, RBAC, Network Controls and Auditing

questions.stem: A company wants to follow the principle of least privilege for daily Snowflake administration tasks. Which system role should be used as the primary role for creating and managing database objects, while ensuring ACCOUNTADMIN is reserved for account-level operations only?

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN, because it has all privileges and can do everything needed
PUBLIC, because it is automatically granted to all users
SYSADMIN, because it is designed to create and manage objects without full account-level access
SECURITYADMIN, because it can manage all roles and users

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because SYSADMIN is specifically designed for creating and owning database objects (databases, schemas, tables, warehouses) and is the recommended role for day-to-day object management, keeping ACCOUNTADMIN reserved for account-level tasks only.

**A** is incorrect because using ACCOUNTADMIN for routine tasks violates least privilege. ACCOUNTADMIN has all privileges including billing and account management and should be used sparingly.

**B** is incorrect because PUBLIC is automatically granted to all users and is not appropriate for administrative object creation.

**D** is incorrect because SECURITYADMIN is focused on role and user management (GRANT/REVOKE privileges, CREATE USER), not on creating and managing database objects.

[Snowflake role hierarchy and access control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: An organization creates a custom role hierarchy for their Snowflake environment. A new custom role ANALYST_ROLE is created but NOT granted to SYSADMIN. What is the consequence of this omission?

options.text: SYSADMIN cannot manage or drop objects owned by ANALYST_ROLE, and ACCOUNTADMIN is required to administer those objects
Users with ANALYST_ROLE cannot log into Snowflake
ANALYST_ROLE cannot be granted any privileges on tables
The role will automatically be deleted after 30 days

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because when a custom role is not granted to SYSADMIN (directly or through a chain), SYSADMIN cannot manage objects owned by that role. Only ACCOUNTADMIN or the role itself can then administer those objects. Best practice is to always grant custom roles up to SYSADMIN.

**B** is incorrect because there is no login restriction caused by this omission. Users can still log in and use a role that isn't connected to SYSADMIN.

**C** is incorrect because the disconnected role can still be granted privileges on tables; the omission only affects SYSADMIN's ability to manage owned objects.

**D** is incorrect because Snowflake does not automatically delete unused or disconnected roles after any time period.

[Snowflake custom role hierarchy](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to set a maximum statement execution time of 2 hours (7200 seconds) for all sessions in the account, except for a specific warehouse used by data engineering pipelines which should have no timeout. Which combination of commands accomplishes this?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET LOCK_TIMEOUT = 7200; ALTER WAREHOUSE pipeline_wh SET LOCK_TIMEOUT = 0;
ALTER ACCOUNT SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 7200; ALTER WAREHOUSE pipeline_wh SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 0;
ALTER ACCOUNT SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 7200; DROP PARAMETER STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS ON WAREHOUSE pipeline_wh;
CREATE SESSION POLICY account_timeout SESSION_TIMEOUT = 7200; ALTER WAREHOUSE pipeline_wh UNSET SESSION_POLICY;

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. ALTER ACCOUNT SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 7200 sets the account-wide default, and setting the same parameter to 0 on the specific warehouse overrides that account-level setting for queries running on it, effectively disabling the timeout for the pipeline warehouse.

**A** is incorrect because LOCK_TIMEOUT controls how long a query waits for a table lock, not total statement execution time.

**C** is incorrect because DROP PARAMETER is not a valid Snowflake syntax for clearing a parameter override on a warehouse.

**D** is incorrect because SESSION POLICY controls session idle timeout, not statement execution time, and UNSET SESSION_POLICY removes a session policy, not a statement timeout.

[Snowflake STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS parameter](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/parameters#statement-timeout-in-seconds)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is creating a password policy to enforce corporate security standards. Select TWO requirements that can be enforced using a Snowflake PASSWORD POLICY object. (Select TWO)

options.text: Minimum password length of 14 characters
Password must not match any of the last 10 passwords used
Password expires automatically when the user has not logged in for 90 days
Automatic account lockout after 5 failed login attempts

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. PASSWORD_MIN_LENGTH is a supported parameter of a Snowflake PASSWORD POLICY object, allowing enforcement of a minimum character length requirement.

**B** is correct. PASSWORD_HISTORY is a supported parameter that prevents users from reusing a specified number of previous passwords.

**C** is incorrect. Password expiry based on inactivity is controlled by LOGIN POLICY or the DISABLE_SUSPENDED_USER_ON_LOGIN setting, not by PASSWORD POLICY.

**D** is incorrect. Account lockout after failed login attempts is managed through LOGIN POLICY (MAX_LOGIN_FAILURES parameter), not through PASSWORD POLICY.

[Snowflake password policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-user-management#password-policies)

questions.stem: A company has the following role hierarchy: SYSADMIN -> DATA_OPS_ROLE -> ETL_ROLE. The ETL_ROLE owns a table SALES.PUBLIC.ORDERS. A user with only the DATA_OPS_ROLE active tries to drop the table. What happens?

options.text: The drop fails because privilege inheritance flows upward (from child to parent), so DATA_OPS_ROLE does not inherit ETL_ROLE's OWNERSHIP privilege
The drop succeeds because DATA_OPS_ROLE inherits OWNERSHIP from ETL_ROLE through the hierarchy
The drop succeeds because SYSADMIN has OWNERSHIP over all objects
The drop fails because only ACCOUNTADMIN can drop tables

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. In Snowflake's role hierarchy, privileges flow upward — from granted roles to the roles that contain them. Because DATA_OPS_ROLE has ETL_ROLE granted to it, DATA_OPS_ROLE inherits all privileges held by ETL_ROLE, including OWNERSHIP of the ORDERS table. The drop therefore succeeds.

**A** is incorrect. Privilege inheritance does not flow downward (from parent to child). DATA_OPS_ROLE inherits FROM ETL_ROLE, not the other way around.

**C** is incorrect. SYSADMIN having OWNERSHIP over all objects is a misconception; SYSADMIN inherits from DATA_OPS_ROLE but the ability to drop is from inheritance of the OWNERSHIP privilege, which the question attributes to DATA_OPS_ROLE.

**D** is incorrect. ACCOUNTADMIN is not required to drop tables; any role with OWNERSHIP on the table can drop it.

[Snowflake privilege inheritance](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: An administrator receives a request to create a new user for an application service account with a 90-day key pair authentication setup and no interactive login capability. Which command correctly creates this user?

options.text: CREATE USER svc_app TYPE = SERVICE RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key>' DEFAULT_ROLE = APP_ROLE DISABLED = FALSE;
CREATE USER svc_app NO_PASSWORD RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key>' DEFAULT_ROLE = APP_ROLE;
CREATE USER svc_app TYPE = SERVICE LOGIN_NAME = NULL RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key>';
CREATE USER svc_app PASSWORD = NULL RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key>' DEFAULT_ROLE = APP_ROLE MUST_CHANGE_PASSWORD = FALSE;

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Setting PASSWORD = NULL disables password-based login for the service account, while RSA_PUBLIC_KEY enables key pair authentication. MUST_CHANGE_PASSWORD = FALSE ensures no password-change prompt appears at login, making this the complete setup for a service account.

**A** is incorrect. TYPE = SERVICE is valid syntax in newer Snowflake versions but alone does not guarantee no interactive login without also nulling the password.

**B** is incorrect. TYPE = SERVICE with LOGIN_NAME = NULL is not valid syntax for disabling interactive login.

**C** is incorrect. NO_PASSWORD is not a valid Snowflake CREATE USER parameter.

[Snowflake key pair authentication](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/key-pair-auth)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to implement a session policy to automatically log out idle users after 30 minutes. Select TWO statements that are true about Snowflake session policies. (Select TWO)

options.text: A session policy can be set at the account level using ALTER ACCOUNT SET SESSION_POLICY = policy_name
A session policy set on a specific user overrides the account-level session policy for that user
SESSION_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MINS in a session policy controls both the web UI and programmatic client idle timeouts equally
Session policies can enforce a maximum session duration regardless of activity

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. A session policy can be applied at the account level using ALTER ACCOUNT SET SESSION_POLICY = policy_name, establishing a default for all users in the account.

**B** is correct. A session policy set at the user level with ALTER USER SET SESSION_POLICY overrides the account-level session policy for that specific user.

**C** is incorrect. SESSION_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MINS applies primarily to web UI (SnowSight) sessions; programmatic clients (JDBC, ODBC, Python connector) have separate timeout behaviors and are not identically governed by this parameter.

**D** is incorrect. Snowflake session policies control idle timeout, not absolute maximum session duration regardless of activity.

[Snowflake session policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/session-policies)

questions.stem: Which system role is specifically designed to create and manage users and roles, but does NOT have the ability to modify account-level parameters or manage billing?

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN
SYSADMIN
SECURITYADMIN
USERADMIN

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. USERADMIN is specifically scoped to creating and managing users and roles (CREATE USER, ALTER USER, CREATE ROLE) without access to account-level parameters or billing information.

**A** is incorrect. ACCOUNTADMIN has all privileges including billing and account parameter management.

**B** is incorrect. SYSADMIN creates and manages database objects (databases, schemas, tables, warehouses), not users and roles primarily.

**C** is incorrect. SECURITYADMIN can manage all aspects of access control including granting and revoking any privilege and inherits USERADMIN's capabilities, but it is broader in scope.

[Snowflake system roles](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to be notified when credit consumption on a specific warehouse reaches 75% of its monthly quota, and have all running queries cancelled immediately when it reaches 100%. Which resource monitor trigger configuration achieves this?

options.text: TRIGGERS ON 75 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND
TRIGGERS ON 75 PERCENT DO SUSPEND ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY
TRIGGERS ON 75 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY
TRIGGERS ON 75 PERCENT DO ALERT ON 100 PERCENT DO CANCEL_ALL

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. NOTIFY sends an email alert without affecting warehouse operation at 75%, and SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY at 100% cancels all running queries and immediately prevents new ones from starting.

**A** is incorrect. SUSPEND (not SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY) at 100% would allow currently running queries to finish before suspending, which does not immediately cancel them as required.

**B** is incorrect. Option B skips the notification phase at 75% and goes straight to SUSPEND at 75%, then SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY at 100%.

**D** is incorrect. ALERT and CANCEL_ALL are not valid resource monitor trigger action keywords in Snowflake.

[Snowflake resource monitor trigger actions](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: An organization's ACCOUNTADMIN notices that total Snowflake credit consumption is unexpectedly high this month. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides a breakdown of credit consumption by service type (compute, serverless, cloud services) on a daily basis?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY provides daily aggregated credit consumption broken down by service type — including virtual warehouses, cloud services, and serverless features such as Snowpipe and automatic clustering.

**A** is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY provides hourly per-warehouse data and does not include serverless credits or a cross-service breakdown.

**B** is incorrect. STORAGE_USAGE tracks bytes stored, not credits consumed.

**C** is incorrect. METERING_HISTORY provides hourly warehouse-level data similar to WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY and does not include the daily cross-service breakdown needed.

[Snowflake METERING_DAILY_HISTORY view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/metering_daily_history)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator creates a resource monitor at the account level and assigns it to two specific warehouses. Select TWO true statements about this configuration. (Select TWO)

options.text: Credits consumed by both warehouses count toward the same shared quota on the account-level resource monitor
Credits consumed by serverless features (e.g., Snowpipe) also count toward the resource monitor quota
A warehouse-level resource monitor on one of those warehouses takes precedence over the account-level resource monitor for that warehouse
The resource monitor quota resets at the start of each calendar month by default

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. When a warehouse-level resource monitor is assigned to a specific warehouse, it takes precedence over the account-level resource monitor for that warehouse, which then only applies to warehouses without their own assigned monitor.

**D** is correct. Resource monitor quotas reset at the start of each calendar month by default (FREQUENCY = MONTHLY is the default setting).

**A** is incorrect. Credits from warehouses that have their own warehouse-level resource monitors do not count against the account-level resource monitor's quota.

**B** is incorrect. Serverless feature credits (Snowpipe, automatic clustering) are not tracked by resource monitors; they require the Budget feature for granular control.

[Snowflake resource monitors](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: A company uses automatic clustering on several large tables. The finance team reports unexpectedly high credit costs but no corresponding large warehouse usage. Where should the administrator look to identify the source of these serverless credits?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY filtered by warehouse type = 'STANDARD'
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY filtered by SERVICE_TYPE = 'AUTO_CLUSTERING'
SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS and check the USED_CREDITS column

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY includes a SERVICE_TYPE column that distinguishes AUTO_CLUSTERING credits from warehouse compute and Snowpipe credits. Filtering by SERVICE_TYPE = 'AUTO_CLUSTERING' isolates the source of these unexpected charges.

**A** is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY only captures virtual warehouse credits and does not include serverless automatic clustering credits.

**B** is incorrect. AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY provides table-level detail but is not the primary cross-service cost breakdown view; METERING_DAILY_HISTORY is the better starting point.

**D** is incorrect. SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS tracks credits consumed by virtual warehouses assigned to the monitor, not serverless features like automatic clustering.

[Snowflake METERING_DAILY_HISTORY and AUTO_CLUSTERING](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/metering_daily_history)

questions.stem: A resource monitor with CREDIT_QUOTA = 500 and FREQUENCY = WEEKLY is assigned to a warehouse. On Wednesday, the warehouse has consumed 480 credits. The trigger is set to SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY at 100%. A long-running query is executing when the 500-credit threshold is hit. What happens to that query?

options.text: The query is immediately cancelled and the warehouse is suspended
The query completes normally because it was already running before the threshold was hit
The query is queued and will run when the weekly quota resets
The query runs for an additional grace period of 1 hour before being cancelled

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY is the most aggressive trigger action — it cancels ALL queries currently running on the warehouse and immediately suspends it, regardless of whether they were in progress when the threshold was crossed.

**B** is incorrect. SUSPEND (without IMMEDIATELY) allows running queries to complete before suspending the warehouse; it does not cancel in-flight queries.

**C** is incorrect. Queries are not queued for the next quota period. They must be manually resubmitted after the quota resets and the warehouse is resumed.

**D** is incorrect. There is no grace period associated with SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY; cancellation is immediate upon the threshold being reached.

[Snowflake resource monitor SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY action](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: A data analyst complains they cannot query a table in the SALES schema even though they have SELECT privileges on the table. After investigation, the administrator finds the analyst's role has no USAGE privilege on the SALES schema. Which statement fixes this without granting unnecessary additional privileges?

options.text: GRANT ALL ON SCHEMA SALES TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE;
GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE SALES_DB TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE;
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA SALES TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE;
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA SALES TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE;

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA SALES TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE is the targeted fix. To access objects within a schema, a role must have USAGE on both the containing database and the schema. The scenario states the analyst lacks USAGE on the schema.

**A** is incorrect. GRANT ALL ON SCHEMA grants excessive privileges and violates the principle of least privilege.

**B** is incorrect. GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE is also necessary for the full access chain but was not identified as the missing grant in this scenario; the schema-level USAGE is the specific gap.

**D** is incorrect. The analyst already has SELECT on the tables per the scenario; re-granting SELECT does not address the missing schema USAGE.

[Snowflake USAGE privilege on schemas](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-privileges)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator creates a schema using CREATE SCHEMA analytics WITH MANAGED ACCESS. A table REPORTS is created in this schema. Which role can grant SELECT on REPORTS to other roles?

options.text: The role that owns the REPORTS table (the object owner)
Only the schema owner or SECURITYADMIN (which owns the schema)
Any role with USAGE on the analytics schema
Any role with the MANAGE GRANTS privilege

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. In a managed access schema (WITH MANAGED ACCESS), the schema owner or a role with the MANAGE GRANTS privilege (effectively SECURITYADMIN or ACCOUNTADMIN) is the only one who can grant privileges on objects within the schema. Object owners lose this ability.

**A** is incorrect. In a managed access schema, object owners explicitly cannot grant privileges on their own objects — this is the key purpose of managed access.

**C** is incorrect. Holding USAGE on the schema does not grant the ability to grant privileges on objects within it.

**D** is incorrect. While roles with MANAGE GRANTS can grant privileges, the schema owner is the typical and designated grantor in a managed access schema; MANAGE GRANTS alone is not the full picture provided in this option.

[Snowflake managed access schemas](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview#managed-access-schemas)

questions.stem: An administrator needs to ensure that any new table created in the REPORTING schema automatically gets SELECT granted to the BI_ROLE. Select TWO commands that together accomplish this for both existing and future tables. (Select TWO)

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA REPORTING TO ROLE BI_ROLE;
GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA REPORTING TO ROLE BI_ROLE;
ALTER SCHEMA REPORTING SET DEFAULT_GRANTS = SELECT TO ROLE BI_ROLE;
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA REPORTING TO ROLE BI_ROLE WITH GRANT OPTION;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA REPORTING TO ROLE BI_ROLE covers all tables that exist at the moment the command executes, providing access to current tables.

**B** is correct. GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA REPORTING TO ROLE BI_ROLE automatically grants SELECT on any new tables created in the schema going forward, providing forward coverage.

**C** is incorrect. ALTER SCHEMA SET DEFAULT_GRANTS is not valid Snowflake syntax.

**D** is incorrect. GRANT USAGE WITH GRANT OPTION on the schema allows BI_ROLE to grant schema USAGE to others, but does not automatically grant SELECT on tables and is unrelated to the requirement.

[Snowflake FUTURE grants](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/grant-privilege)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to transfer ownership of a table from ROLE_A to ROLE_B, but wants to revoke all existing grants that ROLE_A made on that table. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE mydb.myschema.orders TO ROLE ROLE_B;
GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE mydb.myschema.orders TO ROLE ROLE_B COPY CURRENT GRANTS;
GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE mydb.myschema.orders TO ROLE ROLE_B REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS;
ALTER TABLE mydb.myschema.orders TRANSFER OWNERSHIP TO ROLE_B REVOKE ALL GRANTS;

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE mydb.myschema.orders TO ROLE ROLE_B REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS transfers ownership to ROLE_B and simultaneously revokes all previously granted access privileges on the table. This is the explicit, clean transfer syntax.

**A** is incorrect. Without REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS or COPY CURRENT GRANTS, the default behavior may vary; REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS is the unambiguous form when the requirement is to remove existing grants.

**B** is incorrect. COPY CURRENT GRANTS preserves all existing privileges granted by the previous owner, which is the opposite of what is required here.

**D** is incorrect. ALTER TABLE TRANSFER OWNERSHIP is not valid Snowflake syntax.

[Snowflake GRANT OWNERSHIP with REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/grant-ownership)

questions.stem:
A security team requires that users from the SALES_TEAM role can only see rows in the CUSTOMERS table where the REGION column matches their own region assignment, stored in a mapping table. A row access policy is created as follows:

CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY region_policy AS (region_col VARCHAR) RETURNS BOOLEAN ->
  EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM region_mapping WHERE user_region = region_col AND username = CURRENT_USER());

The policy is applied. A user with ACCOUNTADMIN role queries CUSTOMERS. What rows does ACCOUNTADMIN see?

options.text: All rows, because row access policies do not apply to ACCOUNTADMIN
All rows, because ACCOUNTADMIN automatically bypasses all security policies
No rows, because ACCOUNTADMIN is not in the region_mapping table
Only rows where the user's name appears in region_mapping, same as any other user

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Row access policies in Snowflake apply to ALL roles including ACCOUNTADMIN. The policy evaluates CURRENT_USER() against the region_mapping table. If ACCOUNTADMIN's username does not appear in that table, the EXISTS subquery returns false and ACCOUNTADMIN sees zero rows.

**A** is incorrect. Row access policies do not have a special bypass for ACCOUNTADMIN; unlike some database systems, Snowflake applies these policies universally.

**B** is incorrect. "All rows, because row access policies do not apply to ACCOUNTADMIN" is factually wrong — policies apply to all roles.

**C** is incorrect. ACCOUNTADMIN does not automatically bypass all security policies; it is subject to row access policies like any other role.

[Snowflake row access policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-row-intro)

questions.stem: A company wants to restrict Snowflake access to only their corporate IP range (192.168.10.0/24) and explicitly block a known suspicious IP (203.0.113.50). Which command creates a network policy that achieves this?

options.text: CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy ALLOW = ('192.168.10.0/24') DENY = ('203.0.113.50');
CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('192.168.10.0/24') BLOCKED_IP_LIST = ('203.0.113.50');
CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy WHITELIST = ('192.168.10.0/24') BLACKLIST = ('203.0.113.50');
CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy IP_ALLOWLIST ('192.168.10.0/24') IP_BLOCKLIST ('203.0.113.50');

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. The correct Snowflake CREATE NETWORK POLICY syntax uses ALLOWED_IP_LIST and BLOCKED_IP_LIST parameters. BLOCKED_IP_LIST takes precedence over ALLOWED_IP_LIST for any overlapping entries.

**A** is incorrect. ALLOW and DENY are not valid Snowflake network policy parameter names.

**C** is incorrect. WHITELIST and BLACKLIST are not valid Snowflake network policy parameter names; Snowflake deprecated these terms in favor of ALLOWED_IP_LIST and BLOCKED_IP_LIST.

**D** is incorrect. The syntax without equals signs (IP_ALLOWLIST without =) is not valid Snowflake DDL.

[Snowflake network policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: A company has set a network policy at the account level restricting access to a corporate VPN IP range. An individual developer needs access from a personal IP that is outside the corporate range for an emergency. How can an administrator grant this without changing the account-level policy?

options.text: Temporarily disable the account-level network policy using ALTER ACCOUNT UNSET NETWORK_POLICY
Add the developer's IP to the account-level ALLOWED_IP_LIST temporarily
Create a separate network policy that includes the developer's IP and assign it to that specific user with ALTER USER SET NETWORK_POLICY
Network policies cannot be scoped to individual users; the account-level policy must be modified

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Snowflake supports network policies at both the account and user level. A user-level network policy overrides the account-level policy for that specific user. The administrator creates a new policy including the developer's IP and assigns it with ALTER USER developer_name SET NETWORK_POLICY = dev_policy.

**A** is incorrect. Disabling the account-level policy removes IP restrictions for all users, which is a security risk and not the targeted approach needed.

**B** is incorrect. Adding the developer's IP to the account-level ALLOWED_IP_LIST changes the policy for all users, not just the individual developer.

**D** is incorrect. Network policies can be scoped to individual users; this is a documented and supported feature.

[Snowflake user-level network policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: A company operating on AWS is implementing AWS PrivateLink for Snowflake. Select TWO accurate statements about this configuration. (Select TWO)

options.text: After enabling PrivateLink, all clients must use a new account URL that includes 'privatelink' in the hostname
AWS PrivateLink for Snowflake is available on all Snowflake editions including Standard
Traffic between the VPC and Snowflake over PrivateLink traverses the public internet but is encrypted
PrivateLink requires the Snowflake account and the VPC to be in the same AWS region

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. When AWS PrivateLink is configured, clients must use the PrivateLink-specific account URL (e.g., account.privatelink.snowflakecomputing.com). The standard public URL will not route traffic through PrivateLink.

**D** is correct. The Snowflake account and the AWS VPC must be in the same AWS region for PrivateLink connectivity to work.

**B** is incorrect. PrivateLink is available only on Business Critical edition and above, not on Standard edition.

**C** is incorrect. The primary benefit of PrivateLink is that traffic does NOT traverse the public internet — it stays on the AWS backbone network.

[Snowflake AWS PrivateLink](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-privatelink)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is rotating key pair authentication credentials for a service account without causing any downtime. The service account currently has RSA_PUBLIC_KEY set. What is the correct rotation procedure?

options.text: DROP USER svc_acct; CREATE USER svc_acct RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<new_key>';
ALTER USER svc_acct SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<new_key>'; (This replaces the old key immediately)

ALTER USER svc_acct SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 = '<new_key>'; (Update application to use new key, then run: ALTER USER svc_acct UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY; ALTER USER svc_acct SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<new_key>'; UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2;)
ALTER USER svc_acct ROTATE RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<new_key>';

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Snowflake supports two RSA public key slots (RSA_PUBLIC_KEY and RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2) for zero-downtime rotation. The procedure is: (1) set the new key in RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2, (2) update the application to use the new key, (3) verify it works, then (4) remove the old key. This ensures the service never has a moment where neither key is valid.

**A** is incorrect. Dropping and recreating the user loses all associated role grants and is highly disruptive.

**B** is incorrect. ALTER USER SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY replaces the existing key immediately, causing downtime for any application that is still using the old key during the switch.

**D** is incorrect. ALTER USER ROTATE RSA_PUBLIC_KEY is not valid Snowflake syntax.

[Snowflake key pair rotation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/key-pair-auth#rotating-key-pairs)

questions.stem: A warehouse has AUTO_SUSPEND set to 60 seconds and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE. A batch job submits queries every 5 minutes. What is the expected behavior?

options.text: The warehouse stays running continuously because queries arrive before the 60-second idle threshold expires
The warehouse will remain suspended after the first query completes because the batch job ends
AUTO_RESUME only works if a human manually resumes the warehouse first
The warehouse suspends between each batch run and auto-resumes for each query, charging for each resume cycle

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. With AUTO_SUSPEND = 60 seconds and queries arriving every 5 minutes, the warehouse idles for 60 seconds after each batch and then suspends. When the next query arrives 5 minutes later — well past the 60-second threshold — AUTO_RESUME = TRUE triggers an automatic restart. Each resume incurs a minimum billing charge.

**A** is incorrect. The warehouse does not stay running continuously because the 5-minute interval between queries exceeds the 60-second auto-suspend threshold.

**B** is incorrect. AUTO_RESUME works automatically for any client connection without requiring manual intervention.

**C** is incorrect. The warehouse will not remain permanently suspended; AUTO_RESUME = TRUE ensures it starts when a new query is submitted.

[Snowflake AUTO_SUSPEND and AUTO_RESUME](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-considerations)

questions.stem: A data science team runs very large Snowpark ML model training jobs that frequently run out of memory on a standard extra-large warehouse. What warehouse type should the administrator provision to address this?

options.text: A STANDARD warehouse with SCALING_POLICY = ECONOMY to maximize memory across nodes
A SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED warehouse, which provides 16x more memory per node than a standard warehouse of the same size
A multi-cluster STANDARD warehouse with MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 4 to distribute the memory workload
A STANDARD warehouse with QUERY_ACCELERATION enabled to extend memory capacity on demand

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED warehouses provide 16x more memory per node compared to standard warehouses of the same size, making them purpose-built for Snowpark ML training jobs, pandas on Snowflake, and other memory-intensive workloads.

**A** is incorrect. SCALING_POLICY = ECONOMY on a standard warehouse controls multi-cluster scaling behavior for concurrency, not per-node memory capacity.

**C** is incorrect. Multi-cluster warehouses scale by adding more clusters for concurrent users/queries, not by increasing per-query memory on individual nodes.

**D** is incorrect. Query Acceleration Service offloads portions of large data scans to serverless compute for better performance, but does not increase warehouse memory.

[Snowflake Snowpark-optimized warehouses](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-snowpark-optimized)

questions.stem: A DBA wants to understand the clustering efficiency of a large table before enabling automatic clustering. Which TWO commands can provide information about the table's current clustering state? (Select TWO)

options.text: SELECT SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION('mydb.myschema.orders', '(order_date, customer_id)');
SHOW CLUSTER KEYS IN TABLE mydb.myschema.orders;
SELECT * FROM TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.CLUSTERING_INFORMATION('mydb.myschema.orders'));
SELECT SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_DEPTH('mydb.myschema.orders', '(order_date, customer_id)');

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION returns a detailed JSON object with clustering metrics including average_depth, average_overlap, and partition statistics for the specified column expressions.

**D** is correct. SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_DEPTH returns just the average clustering depth as a scalar value, useful for quick comparisons and threshold checks.

**B** is incorrect. SHOW CLUSTER KEYS shows which clustering keys are defined on the table but provides no depth or efficiency metrics.

**C** is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.CLUSTERING_INFORMATION used as a table function is not valid syntax; the clustering information functions are system functions, not INFORMATION_SCHEMA table functions.

[Snowflake clustering information functions](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/functions/system_clustering_information)

questions.stem: A table has DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 set at the table level, but the database has DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 7. A row is accidentally deleted. Can Time Travel recover this data?

options.text: No, because the table-level setting of 0 takes precedence and disables Time Travel for this specific table
Yes, because the database-level setting of 7 days overrides the table-level setting of 0
Yes, but only within a 1-hour window because Snowflake has a minimum retention of 1 hour
No, but Fail-Safe can recover data for up to 7 days after deletion even with retention set to 0

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. In Snowflake, the most specific (lowest) level setting takes precedence. A table-level DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 explicitly disables Time Travel for that table, overriding the database-level setting of 7 days. With retention = 0, there is no Time Travel history to query.

**B** is incorrect. The database-level setting does not override a more specific table-level setting; hierarchy flows from account → database → schema → table, with the most specific level winning.

**C** is incorrect. Snowflake does not have a minimum 1-hour retention override that bypasses an explicit 0-day setting.

**D** is incorrect. Fail-Safe is a separate protection layer managed by Snowflake Support, but a table with DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 also loses Fail-Safe coverage per Snowflake documentation.

[Snowflake Time Travel data retention](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel)

questions.stem: A compliance team needs to audit which users accessed a specific table containing PII data over the past 6 months. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides column-level access tracking with sufficient historical retention?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. ACCESS_HISTORY provides table- and column-level access records showing which users accessed which objects (reads and writes), retaining data for 1 year — ideal for a 6-month PII access audit.

**A** is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY shows query text and metadata but does not provide structured column-level access lineage with identified object references.

**C** is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY retains only 7 days of data, which is insufficient for a 6-month audit.

**D** is incorrect. LOGIN_HISTORY tracks authentication events (successful and failed logins) but not data object access.

[Snowflake ACCESS_HISTORY view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/access_history)

questions.stem: A security audit requires a list of all active masking policies and the tables/columns they are currently applied to. Which approach provides this information most directly?

options.text: Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.POLICY_REFERENCES filtered by POLICY_KIND = 'MASKING_POLICY'
Run SHOW MASKING POLICIES and manually cross-reference with SHOW COLUMNS for each table
Query SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.POLICY_REFERENCES() table function per schema
Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY and filter for masking policy events

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.POLICY_REFERENCES is a view that shows all policy associations across the account, including which masking policies are applied to which table columns. Filtering by POLICY_KIND = 'MASKING_POLICY' gives a direct, account-wide list.

**B** is incorrect. SHOW MASKING POLICIES lists policy definitions but not their column associations; manual cross-referencing would be time-consuming and error-prone.

**C** is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.POLICY_REFERENCES() is a per-database table function with 7-day retention, less comprehensive than ACCOUNT_USAGE for an account-wide audit.

**D** is incorrect. ACCESS_HISTORY tracks which objects were accessed by queries, not which policies are associated with which columns.

[Snowflake POLICY_REFERENCES view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/policy_references)

questions.stem: An administrator uses SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS and sees a monitor with STATUS = 'SUSPENDED'. What does this indicate?

options.text: The resource monitor itself has been disabled by an administrator and is not tracking credits
The monitor has detected a billing anomaly and suspended itself for safety
The quota period has ended and the monitor is waiting for the next reset cycle
One or more warehouses assigned to this monitor have been suspended due to the monitor's trigger action

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. In SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS, a STATUS of SUSPENDED means that the resource monitor triggered a SUSPEND or SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY action and one or more assigned warehouses were suspended because a credit threshold was reached. The monitor itself continues to track credits.

**A** is incorrect. A SUSPENDED status means warehouses were suspended by the monitor, not that the monitor itself was disabled. A disabled monitor would show a different status.

**B** is incorrect. The quota period reset cycle would result in the counter resetting, not a SUSPENDED status.

**C** is incorrect. Resource monitors do not self-suspend due to billing anomaly detection; suspension is triggered only by configured credit threshold actions.

[Snowflake resource monitor status](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: A user reports they cannot see data in a view that they previously had access to. The administrator runs SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE user_role and confirms the role has SELECT on the view. Select TWO additional checks the administrator should perform to diagnose the issue. (Select TWO)

options.text: Verify the user's role has USAGE privilege on the database and schema containing the view
Check whether the view's underlying base tables have a row access policy that now excludes the user
Confirm the user has not exceeded their session credit quota
Run GRANT SELECT ON VIEW to refresh the privilege cache

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Missing USAGE privilege on the database or schema is a common cause of access loss. If the database or schema was recently reorganized or USAGE was revoked, the user can no longer navigate to the view even with SELECT granted.

**B** is correct. A row access policy on the base tables may have changed and now excludes the user, causing the view to return zero rows — which presents as an access issue even though the SELECT privilege is intact.

**C** is incorrect. There is no session credit quota in Snowflake that affects query execution. Credit quotas through resource monitors affect warehouse availability, not individual query permission.

**D** is incorrect. Re-granting SELECT does not refresh a privilege cache. Snowflake evaluates privileges in real time, and re-granting an existing privilege has no effect.

[Snowflake row access policies and privilege troubleshooting](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-row-intro)

questions.stem: An administrator runs ALTER ACCOUNT SET LOCK_TIMEOUT = 3600. Which behavior does this parameter control?

options.text: It defines the session idle timeout before a user is automatically logged out
It limits the total execution time of any SQL statement to 3600 seconds
It sets how long (in seconds) a query will wait to acquire a table lock before returning an error
It controls how long a suspended warehouse will remain in the suspended state

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. LOCK_TIMEOUT specifies the maximum number of seconds a SQL statement will wait to acquire a lock on a resource (such as a table lock during DML). If the lock cannot be obtained within this duration, the statement returns an error.

**A** is incorrect. Session idle timeout is controlled by session policies and the SESSION_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MINS parameter, not LOCK_TIMEOUT.

**B** is incorrect. STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS (not LOCK_TIMEOUT) limits total statement execution time.

**D** is incorrect. Warehouse suspension duration is controlled by AUTO_SUSPEND, not LOCK_TIMEOUT.

[Snowflake LOCK_TIMEOUT parameter](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/parameters#lock-timeout)

questions.stem: An administrator wants to inspect the current value and scope of the LOCK_TIMEOUT parameter at the account level. Which command provides this?

options.text: DESCRIBE PARAMETER LOCK_TIMEOUT;
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCOUNT_PARAMETERS WHERE NAME = 'LOCK_TIMEOUT';
SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'LOCK%' IN ACCOUNT;
SHOW ACCOUNT PARAMETERS WHERE NAME = 'LOCK_TIMEOUT';

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'LOCK%' IN ACCOUNT lists all account-level parameters matching the LIKE pattern, including LOCK_TIMEOUT. The output shows the current value, default, the level at which it was set, and its type.

**A** is incorrect. DESCRIBE PARAMETER is not valid Snowflake syntax; the correct command is SHOW PARAMETERS.

**B** is incorrect. SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE does not have an ACCOUNT_PARAMETERS view for querying parameter values.

**D** is incorrect. SHOW ACCOUNT PARAMETERS with a WHERE clause is not standard Snowflake syntax; the LIKE filter is the supported approach.

[Snowflake SHOW PARAMETERS command](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/show-parameters)

questions.stem: A user's account has been locked due to too many failed login attempts. The administrator needs to immediately unlock the account. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: ALTER USER locked_user SET DISABLED = FALSE;
ALTER USER locked_user RESET LOCKOUT;
GRANT LOGIN TO USER locked_user;
ALTER USER locked_user SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0;

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. ALTER USER locked_user SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0 immediately unlocks the account by setting the remaining lockout time to zero minutes, allowing the user to log in again right away.

**A** is incorrect. DISABLED = FALSE re-enables a user account that was administratively disabled, but it does not clear a lockout state caused by failed login attempts.

**B** is incorrect. GRANT LOGIN TO USER is not valid Snowflake syntax.

**C** is incorrect. ALTER USER RESET LOCKOUT is not valid Snowflake syntax.

[Snowflake user lockout and MINS_TO_UNLOCK](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/alter-user)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is creating a new user for a business analyst. The analyst should automatically connect to the ANALYTICS_WH warehouse and use the ANALYST_DB.PUBLIC schema by default. Select TWO user properties that configure this login experience. (Select TWO)

options.text: DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE = 'ANALYTICS_WH'
DEFAULT_NAMESPACE = 'ANALYST_DB.PUBLIC'
AUTO_CONNECT_WAREHOUSE = 'ANALYTICS_WH'
DEFAULT_SCHEMA = 'ANALYST_DB.PUBLIC'

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE = 'ANALYTICS_WH' sets the warehouse that is automatically activated when the user logs in, eliminating the need for the user to run USE WAREHOUSE.

**B** is correct. DEFAULT_NAMESPACE = 'ANALYST_DB.PUBLIC' specifies the default database and schema context (in DB.SCHEMA format) so the user does not need to run USE DATABASE or USE SCHEMA on login.

**C** is incorrect. AUTO_CONNECT_WAREHOUSE is not a valid Snowflake user property name.

**D** is incorrect. DEFAULT_SCHEMA is not a valid Snowflake user property; the correct property for schema defaulting is DEFAULT_NAMESPACE, which accepts the full DB.SCHEMA format.

[Snowflake user default properties](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/create-user)

questions.stem: A governance team wants to add a description to custom roles to document their purpose. Which command adds a comment to a role named FINANCE_READ_ROLE?

options.text: ALTER ROLE finance_read_role SET DESCRIPTION = 'Read-only access to finance schemas';
ALTER ROLE finance_read_role SET COMMENT = 'Read-only access to finance schemas';
COMMENT ON ROLE finance_read_role IS 'Read-only access to finance schemas';
UPDATE ROLE finance_read_role COMMENT = 'Read-only access to finance schemas';

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. The correct Snowflake syntax is ALTER ROLE role_name SET COMMENT = '...'. The COMMENT property is displayed when running SHOW ROLES and supports governance and documentation workflows.

**A** is incorrect. DESCRIPTION is not a valid property for a Snowflake role; Snowflake uses COMMENT for role documentation.

**C** is incorrect. COMMENT ON ROLE follows a different SQL dialect (standard SQL DCL) that is not used in Snowflake; Snowflake uses the ALTER object SET COMMENT syntax.

**D** is incorrect. UPDATE ROLE is not valid Snowflake DDL syntax; Snowflake uses ALTER for modifying existing objects.

[Snowflake ALTER ROLE command](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/alter-role)

questions.stem: A stored procedure needs to determine the name of the Snowflake task that is currently executing it in order to log task context to an audit table. Which system function returns this information?

options.text: TASK_CONTEXT()
CURRENT_TASK()
SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME()
SYSTEM$TASK_NAME()

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME() is the Snowflake system function that returns the fully-qualified name of the task currently executing a stored procedure or other code, useful for logging and conditional audit logic.

**A** is incorrect. TASK_CONTEXT() is not a valid Snowflake function.

**B** is incorrect. CURRENT_TASK() is not a valid Snowflake function; the correct function name is SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME().

**D** is incorrect. SYSTEM$TASK_NAME() is not a standard Snowflake system function.

[Snowflake SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME function](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/functions/system_current_user_task_name)

questions.stem: An administrator runs a script that creates roles as part of infrastructure provisioning. The script may be run multiple times. Which syntax safely creates a role without failing if it already exists?

options.text: CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS provisioned_role;
CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE provisioned_role;
CREATE ROLE provisioned_role OR IGNORE;
UPSERT ROLE provisioned_role;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS is idempotent — if the role already exists, the command succeeds silently without error and without modifying the existing role or its grants. This is the safe approach for provisioning scripts.

**B** is incorrect. CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE drops and recreates the role, destroying all existing grants to and from that role, which is destructive in a provisioning scenario where the role may already have assignments.

**C** is incorrect. CREATE ROLE ... OR IGNORE is not valid Snowflake syntax.

**D** is incorrect. UPSERT ROLE is not valid Snowflake syntax.

[Snowflake CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/create-role)

questions.stem: An administrator needs to find all roles whose names begin with 'ANALYST'. Which command returns this filtered list?

options.text: SELECT ROLE_NAME FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ROLES WHERE ROLE_NAME LIKE 'ANALYST%';
DESCRIBE ROLES FILTER 'ANALYST%';
LIST ROLES WHERE NAME STARTS WITH 'ANALYST';
SHOW ROLES LIKE 'ANALYST%';

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. SHOW ROLES LIKE 'ANALYST%' filters the role list using SQL LIKE syntax, where % matches any sequence of characters, returning all roles whose names start with 'ANALYST'.

**A** is incorrect. While ACCOUNT_USAGE.ROLES can be queried with SQL and filtered by name, SHOW ROLES LIKE is the direct DDL approach to this task. Note: A is a valid alternative but D is more direct.

**B** is incorrect. LIST ROLES WHERE NAME STARTS WITH is not valid Snowflake syntax.

**C** is incorrect. DESCRIBE ROLES FILTER is not valid Snowflake syntax.

[Snowflake SHOW ROLES command](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/show-roles)

questions.stem: Snowflake periodically releases behavior change bundles that modify existing functionality. An administrator wants to test a specific bundle called '2024_01' in a development account before enabling it in production. Which approach is correct?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE = '2024_01' IN ACCOUNT dev_account;
ALTER SESSION SET ENABLE_BCR_BUNDLES = '2024_01';
SELECT SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('2024_01');
CREATE BEHAVIOR CHANGE TEST BUNDLE = '2024_01';

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. SELECT SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('2024_01') is the system function that enables a specific behavior change bundle in the current account, allowing administrators to opt in on a dev account first and validate impact before production rollout.

**A** is incorrect. ALTER ACCOUNT SET BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE is not valid Snowflake syntax.

**B** is incorrect. ALTER SESSION SET ENABLE_BCR_BUNDLES is not a valid Snowflake session parameter.

**D** is incorrect. CREATE BEHAVIOR CHANGE TEST BUNDLE is not a Snowflake DDL construct.

[Snowflake behavior change bundles](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/release-notes/behavior-change-policy)

questions.stem: An administrator runs SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS and sees one monitor showing CREDIT_QUOTA = 1000 and CREDITS_USED = 423. What does this tell the administrator?

options.text: The CREDITS_USED will reset to 0 every 24 hours automatically
The monitor's triggers have already fired because 423 > 100 (the implicit 100% threshold)
The 423 credits represent only serverless costs; warehouse costs are not reflected here
The monitor has 577 credits remaining before any trigger actions fire

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. CREDITS_USED / CREDIT_QUOTA = 423 / 1000 = 42.3%. The monitor has consumed 42.3% of its quota, leaving 577 credits before the quota is exhausted and any trigger thresholds are reached.

**A** is incorrect. Trigger thresholds are configured at specific PERCENT values by the administrator; 423 credits does not automatically imply any threshold was crossed unless a trigger was set below 42.3%.

**B** is incorrect. CREDITS_USED in a resource monitor tracks virtual warehouse credits, not exclusively serverless costs.

**C** is incorrect. CREDITS_USED resets based on the monitor's configured FREQUENCY period (monthly, weekly, etc.), not every 24 hours.

[Snowflake resource monitor credit tracking](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: A resource monitor has NOTIFY_USERS = ('ADMIN1', 'FINANCE_MGR') configured. When a trigger threshold is reached, who receives the notification email?

options.text: All ACCOUNTADMIN users in the account, plus ADMIN1 and FINANCE_MGR
Only ADMIN1 and FINANCE_MGR, as specified in the NOTIFY_USERS list
The user who created the resource monitor, plus ADMIN1 and FINANCE_MGR
All users with ACCOUNTADMIN or SYSADMIN roles in the account

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. The NOTIFY_USERS list in a resource monitor specifies exactly which Snowflake users receive email notification when a NOTIFY trigger fires. Only those users named in the list are notified, provided they have a valid email configured in their user profile.

**A** is incorrect. NOTIFY_USERS does not automatically include all ACCOUNTADMIN users; when NOTIFY_USERS is specified, only the listed users receive notifications.

**C** is incorrect. The monitor creator is not automatically included in notifications unless their name appears in NOTIFY_USERS.

**D** is incorrect. All ACCOUNTADMIN or SYSADMIN users are not notified by default when a NOTIFY_USERS list is specified; the list takes precedence.

[Snowflake resource monitor NOTIFY_USERS](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: Halfway through the month, a business unit requests an emergency increase to their warehouse's credit quota. The current monitor has CREDIT_QUOTA = 500. The administrator needs to raise it to 750 without resetting the usage counter. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 750;
DROP RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor; CREATE RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor WITH CREDIT_QUOTA = 750;
ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor ADD CREDIT_QUOTA = 250;
UPDATE RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 750;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 750 changes the quota mid-period without resetting the CREDITS_USED counter, preserving the current period's usage tracking while raising the limit.

**B** is incorrect. Dropping and recreating the resource monitor also changes the quota but resets CREDITS_USED to zero, losing the mid-month usage history.

**C** is incorrect. ADD CREDIT_QUOTA is not valid Snowflake resource monitor syntax; the entire new value must be set with SET CREDIT_QUOTA.

**D** is incorrect. UPDATE RESOURCE MONITOR is not valid Snowflake syntax; the correct command is ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR.

[Snowflake ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/alter-resource-monitor)

questions.stem: A team is concerned about query queuing during peak hours on a shared warehouse. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view contains data about queued query wait times and warehouse load?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY filtered by QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME > 0
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING provides 5-minute interval snapshots of warehouse load metrics including the number of queries running, queued, and blocked — specifically designed to help identify periods of overload and queuing pressure.

**B** is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY focuses on credit consumption per warehouse over time, not on the concurrency and queuing metrics needed to diagnose load.

**C** is incorrect. While QUERY_HISTORY has a QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME column for individual queries, it does not provide the aggregate warehouse-level load view that WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING offers.

**D** is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY tracks lifecycle events such as warehouse starts, suspensions, and resumes, not query concurrency or queuing metrics.

[Snowflake WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/warehouse_load_history)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to understand how storage consumption has changed month-over-month over the past year. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides this historical storage data?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATABASE_STORAGE_USAGE_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE provides a daily snapshot of total account storage consumption including databases, internal stages, and Snowflake-managed stages, retaining up to 1 year of data for month-over-month trend analysis.

**A** is incorrect. TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS gives a per-table breakdown of current storage (active, Time Travel, Fail-Safe bytes) but is a current-state view rather than a historical time-series.

**B** is incorrect. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY covers compute credits consumed, not storage bytes.

**C** is incorrect. DATABASE_STORAGE_USAGE_HISTORY provides historical storage data at the per-database level, not the consolidated account-level view needed for overall trend analysis.

[Snowflake STORAGE_USAGE view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/storage_usage)

questions.stem: An administrator suspects Snowpipe and serverless task costs are unexpectedly high. Which TWO ACCOUNT_USAGE views should be consulted to identify the source of these serverless charges? (Select TWO)

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY — for serverless task credit consumption per task
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY — for Snowpipe credit consumption per pipe
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY — for all compute credit usage including Snowpipe
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.RESOURCE_MONITOR_HISTORY — for serverless resource monitor triggers

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY contains credit consumption records for each serverless task execution, enabling drill-down by task name, run time, and credits used to identify high-cost tasks.

**B** is correct. PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY records Snowpipe usage and credits consumed per pipe over time, allowing identification of high-volume or costly pipes.

**C** is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY only captures virtual warehouse credits and does not include Snowpipe serverless credits or serverless task credits.

**D** is incorrect. RESOURCE_MONITOR_HISTORY is not a standard ACCOUNT_USAGE view; resource monitor data is available through SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS and the RESOURCE_MONITORS view.

[Snowflake serverless task and Snowpipe usage history](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/serverless_task_history)

questions.stem: An administrator runs: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA sales TO ROLE reporting_role; A new table is created in the SALES schema the following week. Does REPORTING_ROLE automatically have SELECT on the new table?

options.text: Yes, because GRANT ON ALL TABLES applies to all current and future tables in the schema
Yes, but only if the new table is created by the role that owns the schema
No, because GRANT ON ALL TABLES is a one-time bulk grant for tables that exist at execution time; future tables require a separate GRANT ON FUTURE TABLES
No, because schema-level grants are not valid in Snowflake — grants must be made per table

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA is a bulk point-in-time grant covering only the tables that exist when the command executes. Tables created after this command are not automatically covered, which is a common access gap.

**A** is incorrect. GRANT ON ALL TABLES does not apply to future tables; a separate GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES must be issued to cover subsequently created tables.

**B** is incorrect. Schema-level grants (such as USAGE) are valid and necessary in Snowflake, but GRANT ON ALL TABLES does not automatically apply to future tables.

**D** is incorrect. There is no rule that new tables inherit SELECT grants only when created by the schema owner. The FUTURE TABLES grant mechanism is the correct approach regardless of who creates the table.

[Snowflake GRANT ON ALL TABLES vs FUTURE TABLES](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/grant-privilege)

questions.stem: An administrator needs to remove SELECT privilege on a specific table T from ROLE_R without affecting any other privileges ROLE_R has on other objects. Which command achieves this targeted revoke?

options.text: REVOKE ALL ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t FROM ROLE role_r;
GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t TO ROLE sysadmin REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS;
ALTER ROLE role_r REMOVE PRIVILEGE SELECT ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t;
REVOKE SELECT ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t FROM ROLE role_r;

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. REVOKE SELECT ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t FROM ROLE role_r removes only the SELECT privilege on that specific table from the role, leaving all other grants held by role_r on other objects completely intact.

**A** is incorrect. REVOKE ALL ON TABLE removes all privileges (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, REFERENCES) on that table, not just SELECT.

**B** is incorrect. ALTER ROLE REMOVE PRIVILEGE is not valid Snowflake syntax; REVOKE is the correct command for removing privileges.

**C** is incorrect. GRANT OWNERSHIP REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS transfers ownership and revokes existing privilege grants on the object; it is an ownership operation, not a targeted privilege revoke.

[Snowflake REVOKE privilege](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/revoke-privilege)

questions.stem: A stored procedure is created with EXECUTE AS OWNER. Inside the procedure, the developer uses IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('FINANCE_ROLE') to check if the calling user has a certain role. What does this function return in the caller's rights context?

options.text: It always returns TRUE because the procedure runs as the owner, who has all privileges
It returns TRUE if the role that invoked the stored procedure has FINANCE_ROLE granted to it (directly or through hierarchy)
It returns FALSE because stored procedures cannot inspect the caller's role grants
It returns TRUE only if FINANCE_ROLE is the current primary role of the invoking user

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('role_name') is specifically designed for use inside stored procedures running with EXECUTE AS OWNER to check whether the calling user's active role hierarchy includes the specified role. It returns TRUE if the invoker's role chain includes FINANCE_ROLE, enabling the procedure to make access decisions based on the caller's actual roles.

**A** is incorrect. IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE does not always return TRUE; it evaluates the invoker's specific role hierarchy, not the owner's privileges.

**C** is incorrect. Stored procedures with EXECUTE AS OWNER can inspect the caller's role grants specifically through IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE; the function exists precisely for this purpose.

**D** is incorrect. IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE checks the entire role hierarchy of the invoker, not just the current primary role. It returns TRUE if FINANCE_ROLE is anywhere in the invoker's active role chain.

[Snowflake IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE function](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/functions/is_granted_to_invoker_role)

questions.stem: A security review flags a concern: a role has been granted OWNERSHIP on a production table. Why is OWNERSHIP privilege considered a higher security risk than SELECT or INSERT?

options.text: The OWNERSHIP privilege allows the holder to re-grant any privilege on the owned object to any role, including itself
The OWNERSHIP privilege allows the holder to read all data in the table, bypassing row access policies
OWNERSHIP automatically grants ACCOUNTADMIN-level access to the entire account
OWNERSHIP is permanent and cannot be transferred back to the original owner

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. OWNERSHIP privilege grants full control over an object, including the ability to GRANT and REVOKE any privilege on that object to any other role — effectively allowing the owner to share the object with any role in the system. This privilege escalation risk is why OWNERSHIP should be tightly controlled.

**B** is incorrect. OWNERSHIP does not bypass row access policies; those apply to all roles including object owners.

**C** is incorrect. OWNERSHIP of a table does not grant ACCOUNTADMIN-level access to the entire account; it is scoped to the specific owned object.

**D** is incorrect. OWNERSHIP can be transferred using GRANT OWNERSHIP; it is not permanent.

[Snowflake OWNERSHIP privilege](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-privileges)

questions.stem: A Snowflake account has a database DB_SHARED that was shared from another account via a data share. A role CONSUMER_ROLE needs to query objects in this shared database. Which grant is required that differs from a normal local database?

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE db_shared TO ROLE consumer_role;
GRANT IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE db_shared TO ROLE consumer_role;
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN DATABASE db_shared TO ROLE consumer_role;
GRANT REFERENCE_USAGE ON DATABASE db_shared TO ROLE consumer_role;

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. For databases created from an inbound data share, the privilege model differs from local databases. Instead of USAGE on the database plus individual object grants, a single GRANT IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE gives the role access to all objects within the shared database as defined by the share's original privileges.

**A** is incorrect. GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE is the normal privilege for local databases, but it is not the correct privilege for a shared database imported from a data share.

**C** is incorrect. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES is valid for local tables, but shared databases require IMPORTED PRIVILEGES — you cannot directly grant SELECT on individual tables in a shared database.

**D** is incorrect. GRANT REFERENCE_USAGE is used for cross-database views that reference another database, not for accessing a shared database from a data share.

[Snowflake IMPORTED PRIVILEGES for data sharing](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro)

questions.stem: An administrator needs to view all privileges currently granted on a specific table PROD.SALES.ORDERS to understand who has access. Which command provides this information?

options.text: SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE prod.sales.orders;
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES WHERE NAME = 'ORDERS';
DESCRIBE TABLE prod.sales.orders PRIVILEGES;
SELECT * FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.OBJECT_PRIVILEGES WHERE OBJECT_NAME = 'ORDERS';

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE prod.sales.orders lists all privilege grants on that specific table — which roles have SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and OWNERSHIP — along with who granted each privilege and when. This is the most direct command for auditing access to a specific object.

**B** is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES can be filtered by table name and contains this information, but it has a latency delay (up to 120 minutes) and is less immediate than SHOW GRANTS.

**C** is incorrect. DESCRIBE TABLE PRIVILEGES is not valid Snowflake syntax.

**D** is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.OBJECT_PRIVILEGES can also provide privilege information as a queryable view, but SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE is the more direct and immediate command for this specific task.

[Snowflake SHOW GRANTS command](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/show-grants)

questions.stem: A Snowflake Native App developer is building an application that uses Application Roles. Select TWO statements that are true about Application Roles in Snowflake Native Apps. (Select TWO)

options.text: Application Roles are defined within a Snowflake Native App and are granted to account roles in the consumer account
Application Roles can be granted directly to users in the consumer account without going through account roles
Application Roles are visible in the consumer account via SHOW APPLICATION ROLES IN APPLICATION app_name
Application Roles automatically inherit all privileges of the SYSADMIN role in the consumer account

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Application Roles are defined by the provider within a Snowflake Native App and are designed to be granted to account roles in the consumer account, enabling consumers to control which users have access to the app's capabilities.

**C** is correct. Application Roles installed in a consumer account are visible using SHOW APPLICATION ROLES IN APPLICATION app_name, allowing consumers to see what access tiers the app provides.

**B** is incorrect. Application Roles cannot be granted directly to individual users; they must be granted to account roles, which are then granted to users through the standard role hierarchy.

**D** is incorrect. Application Roles have only the privileges the app provider defined within the app; they do not automatically inherit any privileges from SYSADMIN or other system roles in the consumer account.

[Snowflake Native App Application Roles](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/app-roles)

questions.stem: An administrator has applied a network policy at the account level. A specific user VENDOR_ACCT needs access from an IP address that is not in the account policy's allowed list. What command assigns a user-level network policy to override the account policy for this user?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY = vendor_policy FOR USER vendor_acct;
CREATE USER vendor_acct NETWORK_POLICY = vendor_policy;
GRANT NETWORK POLICY vendor_policy TO USER vendor_acct;
ALTER USER vendor_acct SET NETWORK_POLICY = vendor_policy;

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. ALTER USER vendor_acct SET NETWORK_POLICY = vendor_policy assigns the network policy at the user level. A user-level network policy fully replaces the account-level policy for that specific user, allowing VENDOR_ACCT to connect from the IPs in vendor_policy.

**A** is incorrect. ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY with a FOR USER clause is not valid Snowflake syntax.

**B** is incorrect. GRANT NETWORK POLICY is not valid Snowflake syntax; network policies are assigned through ALTER USER or ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY.

**C** is incorrect. While CREATE USER can set some parameters, NETWORK_POLICY for an existing user is set with ALTER USER, not by recreating the user.

[Snowflake ALTER USER SET NETWORK_POLICY](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/alter-user)

questions.stem: An administrator wants to see all network policies defined in the account along with their allowed IP lists. Which command provides this?

options.text: SHOW NETWORK POLICIES;
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.NETWORK_POLICIES;
DESCRIBE ALL NETWORK POLICIES;
LIST NETWORK POLICIES IN ACCOUNT;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. SHOW NETWORK POLICIES lists all network policies in the account, including the policy name, ALLOWED_IP_LIST, BLOCKED_IP_LIST, and their current assignments. This is the standard DDL command for viewing policy configurations.

**B** is incorrect. SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE does not have a NETWORK_POLICIES view; network policy definitions are accessed via SHOW NETWORK POLICIES or DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICY.

**C** is incorrect. DESCRIBE ALL NETWORK POLICIES is not valid Snowflake syntax; DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICY works on a single named policy.

**D** is incorrect. LIST NETWORK POLICIES IN ACCOUNT is not valid Snowflake syntax.

[Snowflake SHOW NETWORK POLICIES](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/show-network-policies)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is troubleshooting a failure where an external stage backed by an S3 bucket cannot be accessed. Which system function can help diagnose connectivity and permission issues with the external stage?

options.text: SYSTEM$VALIDATE_STORAGE_INTEGRATION('my_s3_integration')
SYSTEM$CHECK_STAGE_ACCESS('@my_s3_stage')
SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('my_s3_stage')
SELECT * FROM TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGE_VALIDATION('my_s3_stage'));

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('stage_name') validates connectivity between Snowflake and an external stage, testing whether the storage integration or IAM role has the permissions needed to read from and list the target bucket, and returns diagnostic information.

**A** is incorrect. SYSTEM$VALIDATE_STORAGE_INTEGRATION is not a standard Snowflake system function.

**B** is incorrect. SYSTEM$CHECK_STAGE_ACCESS is not a valid Snowflake system function for stage validation.

**D** is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGE_VALIDATION used as a table function is not a valid Snowflake construct.

[Snowflake SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/functions/system_verify_external_stage_connection)

questions.stem: A company has enabled AWS PrivateLink for Snowflake and also has a network policy with ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('10.0.0.0/8'). A client connects via the PrivateLink endpoint from a VPC with private IP 10.5.0.25. Which statement accurately describes the interaction?

options.text: The connection is blocked because PrivateLink connections bypass the network policy IP check entirely, and only specific PrivateLink-enabled roles can connect
PrivateLink traffic bypasses IP-based network policy checks because traffic does not arrive from a public IP; the network policy is effectively ignored for PrivateLink connections
The connection succeeds because 10.5.0.25 is within the 10.0.0.0/8 CIDR range in the network policy
The connection fails because PrivateLink connections must use a separate AWSVPCEID-type network rule, not an IP-based network policy

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. When clients connect via AWS PrivateLink, traffic arrives at Snowflake without a public IP address. Because IP-based network policies evaluate source IP addresses, PrivateLink traffic does not present a routable public IP that matches standard CIDR notation, so the IP-based network policy is effectively bypassed. To enforce controls for PrivateLink traffic, administrators should use AWSVPCEID-type network rules instead.

**A** is incorrect. The connection is not blocked because PrivateLink connections bypass IP-based network policy checks entirely; it is not the case that only special roles can connect via PrivateLink.

**C** is incorrect. While 10.5.0.25 is within the 10.0.0.0/8 CIDR range, the IP policy check is bypassed for PrivateLink connections, making the CIDR match irrelevant to the actual behavior.

**D** is incorrect. PrivateLink connections are not blocked by default; they bypass IP-based policy checks, but the solution is to use AWSVPCEID network rules, not that the connection itself fails.

[Snowflake PrivateLink and network policy interaction](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-privatelink)

questions.stem: A security team wants to allow Snowflake connections only from a specific set of AWS VPC endpoints rather than relying on IP address ranges. Which network rule type should be used?

options.text: TYPE = AWSVPCEID with the VPC endpoint IDs as the value list
TYPE = IPV4 with VPC CIDR ranges
TYPE = HOST with the VPC DNS names
TYPE = VPCENDPOINT with the endpoint service names

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Snowflake Network Rules support TYPE = AWSVPCEID, which accepts AWS VPC endpoint IDs (in the format vpce-xxxxxxxxx) in the VALUE_LIST. This restricts Snowflake access to connections arriving specifically through approved VPC endpoints.

**B** is incorrect. TYPE = IPV4 uses IP address CIDR ranges and does not identify specific VPC endpoints; it cannot distinguish PrivateLink endpoint traffic from other traffic in the same IP range.

**C** is incorrect. TYPE = HOST is used for external function and egress domain rules (controlling outbound connections from Snowflake), not for restricting inbound client connections.

**D** is incorrect. TYPE = VPCENDPOINT is not a valid network rule type in Snowflake; the correct type is AWSVPCEID.

[Snowflake network rules AWSVPCEID type](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-rules)

questions.stem: An administrator wants to disable Time Travel on a large temporary staging table to reduce storage costs. The table is in a schema with DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 7. Which command disables Time Travel specifically for this table?

options.text: ALTER TABLE staging_table SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0;
ALTER SCHEMA staging_schema SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0;
ALTER TABLE staging_table DISABLE TIME_TRAVEL;
DROP TABLE staging_table; CREATE TRANSIENT TABLE staging_table (...);

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. ALTER TABLE staging_table SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 disables Time Travel for that specific table without affecting the schema-level setting. The table-level setting takes precedence over schema and account-level defaults.

**B** is incorrect. Altering the schema's retention setting would affect all tables in the schema, not just the specific staging table, violating the requirement to target only this table.

**C** is incorrect. ALTER TABLE ... DISABLE TIME_TRAVEL is not valid Snowflake syntax; the parameter is controlled via DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS.

**D** is incorrect. Dropping and recreating the table as a TRANSIENT TABLE achieves the goal but requires DDL downtime and loses all data; ALTER TABLE is the non-disruptive approach.

[Snowflake DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS parameter](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel)
