questions.stem: A developer creates a TRANSIENT table for intermediate ETL processing. Compared to a permanent table and a TEMPORARY table, what are the key characteristics of a TRANSIENT table?

options.text: A transient table is session-scoped (dropped at session end) and has no Fail-Safe period, like a temporary table
A transient table persists across sessions like a permanent table, supports 0 or 1 day of Time Travel retention, and has no Fail-Safe period
A transient table persists across sessions, supports up to 90 days of Time Travel, and has a 7-day Fail-Safe period
A transient table is dropped automatically after 24 hours and has no Time Travel or Fail-Safe

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Transient tables persist across sessions like permanent tables, are visible to other users, support 0 or 1 day of Time Travel retention, and have no Fail-Safe period — making them a cost-effective choice for non-critical intermediate ETL data.

**A** is incorrect. Session-scoped tables that are dropped at session end are TEMPORARY tables, not transient tables. Transient tables persist across sessions.

**C** is incorrect. Transient tables support only 0 or 1 day of Time Travel, not up to 90 days. The 7-day Fail-Safe period applies to permanent tables, not transient tables.

**D** is incorrect. Transient tables are not automatically dropped after 24 hours; they persist until explicitly dropped, just like permanent tables.

[Snowflake transient tables](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-temp-transient)

questions.stem: An administrator wants to check the Time Travel retention setting currently configured on several tables in a schema. Which command displays the retention_time column for tables?

options.text: SHOW TERSE TABLES IN SCHEMA mydb.myschema;
SELECT TABLE_NAME, RETENTION_TIME FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA = 'MYSCHEMA';
DESCRIBE DATABASE mydb;
SELECT TABLE_NAME FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA = 'MYSCHEMA';

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES includes a RETENTION_TIME column that reflects the DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS setting for each table, and it can be queried with standard SQL to filter, sort, and compare retention across many tables in a schema.

**A** is incorrect. SHOW TERSE TABLES returns a condensed output that omits most table properties including the retention_time column; the full SHOW TABLES output includes it, but TERSE does not.

**C** is incorrect. DESCRIBE DATABASE returns metadata about the database itself, not individual table properties or retention settings.

**D** is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS contains storage consumption columns (ACTIVE_BYTES, TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES, FAILSAFE_BYTES) but does not expose the configured DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS parameter value.

[Snowflake INFORMATION_SCHEMA TABLES view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/info-schema/tables)

questions.stem: A team has a warehouse running mixed standard and Snowpark workloads. After profiling, they determine the Snowpark jobs need more memory but the standard SQL queries do not. What is the correct approach to address this without over-provisioning for standard queries?

options.text: Increase the warehouse size from XL to 2XL so all queries benefit from the additional memory
Alter the existing warehouse: ALTER WAREHOUSE mixed_wh SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED;
Enable QUERY_ACCELERATION on the existing warehouse — it automatically adds memory for Snowpark workloads
Create a separate SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED warehouse for Snowpark jobs and keep the existing standard warehouse for SQL queries

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. The correct architectural approach is to route Snowpark jobs to a dedicated SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED warehouse (16x more memory per node) while keeping SQL queries on a standard STANDARD warehouse sized appropriately. This avoids paying for excess memory on workloads that don't need it.

**A** is incorrect. Increasing warehouse size to 2XL adds compute for all queries equally and does not selectively increase memory for Snowpark jobs without also over-provisioning for standard SQL.

**B** is incorrect. Query Acceleration Service parallelizes large data scans by offloading to serverless compute, but it does not increase per-node memory for Snowpark workloads.

**C** is incorrect. While ALTER WAREHOUSE SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED may be valid syntax in some Snowflake versions, converting the shared warehouse would impact all workloads equally rather than separating the memory-intensive Snowpark jobs from standard SQL queries.

[Snowflake Snowpark-optimized warehouses](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-snowpark-optimized)

questions.stem: An administrator queries ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS and sees a table with ACTIVE_BYTES = 5GB, TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES = 20GB, and FAILSAFE_BYTES = 15GB. Select TWO true statements about this scenario. (Select TWO)

options.text: The 20GB of TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES is billed at the same per-TB rate as the 5GB of active data
The 15GB of FAILSAFE_BYTES is recoverable by the customer using UNDROP or Time Travel queries
Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 1 on this table would immediately reduce TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES to near zero
Reducing DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS on the table will eventually reduce TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES as old historical versions age out

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Time Travel storage is billed at the same per-TB rate as active storage; the 20GB of TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES contributes directly to storage costs at the standard rate.

**D** is correct. Reducing DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS does not immediately purge Time Travel data. Snowflake's background processes clean up historical versions over time, so TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES will gradually decrease as old versions age out and are reclaimed.

**B** is incorrect. Fail-Safe data is Snowflake-managed and accessible only by Snowflake Support, not by the customer using UNDROP or Time Travel queries.

**C** is incorrect. Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 1 does not immediately reduce TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES to near zero; existing Time Travel data is cleaned up gradually by background processes, not instantly upon the retention change.

[Snowflake TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS and storage billing](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/table_storage_metrics)

questions.stem: A compliance team needs to verify all data that left the Snowflake account through COPY INTO external stages or UNLOAD operations over the past 30 days, for data egress cost and compliance auditing. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides this information?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY filtered by QUERY_TYPE = 'COPY'
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY filtered by QUERY_TYPE = 'UNLOAD'
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STAGE_USAGE_HISTORY

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY records all data transfer events including data transferred to external stages (egress), showing bytes transferred, transfer type, source and destination region, and timestamps — enabling both cost analysis and compliance verification.

**A** is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY captures query execution metadata and the query text but does not provide structured egress tracking with bytes transferred to external stages.

**B** is incorrect. ACCESS_HISTORY tracks which objects were accessed by queries (reads and writes to Snowflake objects), not the volume of data physically transferred out to external locations.

**D** is incorrect. STAGE_USAGE_HISTORY is not a standard ACCOUNT_USAGE view in Snowflake.

[Snowflake DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/data_transfer_history)

questions.stem: A security team wants to identify all active sessions and which client applications (e.g., JDBC driver version, Python connector) are currently connected to the Snowflake account. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides both historical and current session information including client application details?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY — columns: CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID, CLIENT_APPLICATION_VERSION
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS — columns: SESSION_ID, USER_NAME, CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID, CREATED_ON, CLIENT_ENVIRONMENT
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY — columns: SESSION_ID, USER_NAME, CLIENT_TYPE
SHOW SESSIONS; — displays all currently active sessions with client application metadata

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS contains records for both active and historical sessions, including SESSION_ID, USER_NAME, CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID (identifying the client type and version), CREATED_ON, and CLIENT_ENVIRONMENT. This is the designated view for session auditing and tracking client tool usage.

**A** is incorrect. LOGIN_HISTORY records individual login events (successful and failed authentications) but does not provide full session lifecycle tracking or detailed client environment metadata.

**C** is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY contains CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID at the query level but is scoped to individual query executions rather than tracking sessions as a whole, making it less efficient for identifying all connected client applications.

**D** is incorrect. SHOW SESSIONS is not a standard Snowflake DDL command; session information is accessed through ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS.

[Snowflake SESSIONS view](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/sessions)

questions.stem: An organization wants to use a resource monitor to control costs at the organizational level across multiple Snowflake accounts. A Snowflake administrator proposes creating one resource monitor and assigning it to all accounts in the organization. Is this possible?

options.text: No, resource monitors are account-scoped objects; each account requires its own resource monitor, and there is no organization-level resource monitor
Yes, resource monitors can be created at the organization level using the ORGADMIN role and assigned to specific accounts
Yes, by creating the monitor in the organization master account, it automatically applies to all child accounts
Yes, but only if all accounts are on the Business Critical edition

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Resource monitors are account-scoped objects in Snowflake. They can be assigned to specific warehouses within an account or to the account as a whole, but they cannot span multiple accounts. Each account in an organization must have its own resource monitors configured independently.

**B** is incorrect. ORGADMIN cannot create cross-account resource monitors; organization-level spending visibility is provided through the ORGANIZATION_USAGE schema, but ORGADMIN cannot configure resource monitors that apply across accounts.

**C** is incorrect. Creating a resource monitor in an organization master account does not cause it to automatically apply to child accounts; resource monitors are account-scoped and have no cross-account effect.

**D** is incorrect. The Business Critical edition requirement applies to certain security features, not to resource monitors, which are available on all Snowflake editions.

[Snowflake resource monitors scope](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)
